does: GRAIL proposes a token-wise advantage reweighting mechanism for reinforcement learning in LLMs that improves upon sequence-level advantage broadcasting used in GRPO-style methods. Rather than assigning uniform advantages to all tokens in a reasoning trajectory, GRAIL uses gradient-activation saliency to identify which tokens are most locally sensitive to the final answer, then reweights their policy gradients accordingly. This directs stronger optimization signals toward reasoning steps and logical inferences while dampening updates to filler words and flawed reasoning. The method is evaluated on five LLM families (Qwen3, R1-distilled, OctoThinker), consistently achieving 3.60% average accuracy improvement and 3.05% Pass@3 improvement over GRPO without requiring expensive process reward models.
connects: This work extends the recent surge in verifiable reward RL for LLMs, building directly on GRPO and related sequence-level reward training approaches that have become standard for improving mathematical reasoning. It responds to a known limitation of these methods: uniform advantage distribution that fails to distinguish between high-value and low-value tokens in a trajectory. The paper connects to broader literature on gradient-based saliency and attribution methods that identify which model inputs or states most influence outputs, as well as prior work on fine-grained supervision in RL that uses process rewards or step-level feedback. GRAIL offers a middle ground by extracting token importance from gradient information rather than training separate process reward models.
enables: This work points toward more efficient reasoning alignment in LLMs without external process supervision. By showing that saliency-based token reweighting improves reasoning performance, it suggests future directions for intrinsic gradient-based feature attribution to guide learning in other RL domains. The approach opens possibilities for combining GRAIL with other reward structures, extending it to multi-turn dialogue or code generation, and exploring alternative saliency metrics beyond gradient-activation. It also sets up questions about scaling: whether fine-grained token reweighting remains beneficial for larger models, longer reasoning chains, and more complex domains. The method could accelerate development of efficient reasoning LLMs by reducing reliance on costly auxiliary models while maintaining performance gains.
